name: Au Sable River
hook: Michigan's most storied trout river and the birthplace of Trout Unlimited. Sandy, spring-fed, and wadeable, with brook and brown trout in the upper river.
hatches: Hendricksons late April–May, sulphurs and brown drakes in June, then the famous Hex hatch at night from late June into mid-July. Fall is streamer season for the biggest browns.
regs: The "Holy Water" from Burton's Landing to Wakeley Bridge is flies-only, catch-and-release.
stretches: Holy Water for numbers of fish, the South Branch through the Mason Tract for solitude, and below Mio dam for trophy browns.

name: Pere Marquette River
hook: A wild, undammed river with resident browns plus heavy runs of salmon in fall and steelhead through winter and spring.
hatches: Gray drakes and sulphurs May–June, hoppers in August. King salmon August–October; steelhead November through April.
regs: The flies-only, no-kill section runs from the M-37 bridge down to Gleason's Landing.
stretches: The Baldwin-area flies-only water wades well; the lower river fishes best from a drift boat.

name: Manistee River
hook: The Au Sable's quieter twin — classic sand-and-gravel brook and brown trout water in the upper river, big migratory fish below Tippy Dam.
hatches: A similar calendar to the Au Sable: drakes and Hex in the slower reaches late June–July, hoppers in August. Below Tippy: salmon in fall, steelhead November–April.
regs: Artificials-only with special size limits between M-72 and CCC Bridge on the upper river.
stretches: Yellow Trees Landing to CCC Bridge up top; Tippy Dam down to High Bridge for salmon and steelhead.

name: Muskegon River
hook: A big, year-round tailwater below Croton Dam with browns, rainbows, and some of Michigan's heaviest steelhead and salmon runs.
hatches: Caddis and gray drakes May–June for residents; salmon September–October; steelhead November–April with the peak around the spring spawn.
regs: Standard Michigan trout rules on most water — check the current fishing guide for the stretch you're on.
stretches: Croton Dam down through Newaygo: drift-boat water with plenty of wadeable gravel at moderate flows.

name: Huron River
hook: Metro Detroit's home water and one of the best smallmouth float fisheries in the Midwest, with a spring trout section up top.
hatches: Smallmouth from late April through October — topwater in summer low light. Carp on the impoundment flats in summer.
regs: The trout water below the Proud Lake dam has special early-season rules; the rest is standard warm-water regs.
stretches: Proud Lake to Milford for April browns; Hudson Mills to Delhi for smallmouth; below Belleville and Flat Rock dams on the lower river.

name: Rogue River
hook: A tributary of the Grand near Rockford, north of Grand Rapids — a designated trout stream up top with a strong fall/spring steelhead run, and smallmouth in the warmer lower reaches.
hatches: Hendricksons and sulphurs May–June, caddis and hoppers through summer on the trout water. Steelhead run in from the Grand in fall and again in spring; summer runs warm and pushes trout to the coolest, shaded stretches.
regs: The designated trout portion around and above Rockford has gear/season rules — check the Michigan DNR trout guide for your exact stretch; the dam at Rockford is a key steelhead staging spot.
stretches: Above Rockford dam for stream trout; the dam area and downstream for staging steelhead; the lower, warmer river for summer smallmouth.

name: Paint Creek
hook: Oakland County's designated coldwater trout stream, following the Paint Creek Trail through Rochester — small, wadeable, and stocked, with wild browns holding in the shaded stretches.
hatches: Blue-winged olives and Hendricksons in spring, sulphurs and caddis into early summer, terrestrials (ants, beetles, hoppers) in late summer. Summer heat is the real limiter — fish the cool dawn window.
regs: Designated trout stream with gear restrictions — large portions prohibit live bait and have catch-and-release / artificial-only rules. Verify the Michigan DNR regs for your section before fishing.
stretches: The trail crossings — Tienken Rd, Dutton Rd, Silverbell Rd, and the Cider Mill — give easy access; walk upstream from each for deeper, shaded pools.

name: Clinton River
hook: Metro Detroit's other home water — a stocked trout section in the upper river near Yates, warming to a solid smallmouth and pike fishery through the middle and lower reaches to Lake St. Clair.
hatches: Trout on BWOs and caddis spring through early summer in the upper river; smallmouth and pike from May into fall lower down. Steelhead and salmon push up from Lake St. Clair in season.
regs: The Yates-area trout stretch has special regs; the rest is standard warm-water. Check the Michigan DNR guide for the Yates dam section.
stretches: The upper river near Yates for stocked trout; below the dams and through Clinton River Park for smallmouth and pike; the lower river and spillway for run fish.

name: Madison River
hook: "The 50-mile riffle" — fast, bouldery water absolutely stuffed with browns and rainbows.
hatches: Salmonflies late June–early July moving upstream day by day; hoppers in August; streamers in fall.
regs: The stretch between Quake and Ennis lakes is wade-only for much of the season — check current combined regs.
stretches: Quake Lake down to Ennis is the classic water; Bear Trap Canyon for solitude (mind the rattlesnakes).

name: Bighorn River
hook: A tailwater below Yellowtail Dam holding one of the highest trout densities in the country.
hatches: Fishes year-round: midges and baetis spring and fall, PMDs and black caddis mid-to-late summer, sowbugs every single day.
regs: Standard Montana rules; the river corridor crosses the Crow Reservation — stay below the high-water mark.
stretches: The upper 13 miles from Afterbay Dam; three access sites make DIY floats straightforward.

name: South Platte River
hook: Colorado's classic technical tailwater — gin-clear water and educated rainbows that inspect every fly.
hatches: Midges year-round, baetis spring and fall, tricos on summer mornings, PMDs June–July. Think small: 20s and smaller, 6X.
regs: Cheesman Canyon is flies-only catch-and-release; the Deckers water is artificials-only with C&R on most of it.
stretches: Cheesman Canyon via the Gill Trail for the experience; the town water at Deckers for easier access.

name: Frying Pan River
hook: Gold Medal tailwater below Ruedi Reservoir, famous for enormous trout fattened on mysis shrimp.
hatches: Mysis patterns near the dam year-round; green drakes July–September (rare for a tailwater); BWOs in fall.
regs: Flies and lures only, catch-and-release on the upper two miles below the dam.
stretches: Fourteen miles of road-accessible water from the dam down to Basalt; the "Toilet Bowl" pool sits right below the outflow.

name: Green River
hook: Gin-clear tailwater winding under red canyon walls below Flaming Gorge — you can watch every eat happen.
hatches: The cicada fall in late May–June is the event of the year; BWOs in spring, terrestrials all summer.
regs: Artificials-only with slot limits — verify current Utah rules before keeping anything.
stretches: The A section (dam to Little Hole, 7 miles) has a trail the whole way; B section to Browns Park is quieter.

name: San Juan River
hook: A midge factory below Navajo Dam that grows 18-inch rainbows on size-24 flies.
hatches: Midges every day of the year, BWOs fall through spring, annelid patterns after flow bumps.
regs: The Quality Water below the dam is single barbless artificials with a one-fish limit — verify current rules.
stretches: Texas Hole and the braids near the dam are famous for a reason; walk downstream for space.

name: Deschutes River
hook: A big desert-canyon river with hard-fighting "redside" rainbows and a summer steelhead run.
hatches: The salmonfly and golden stone hatch in May–June is legendary; caddis all summer; steelhead August–November.
regs: No fishing from a floating device — you must anchor or beach and wade.
stretches: The Maupin day water is the easy entry; Warm Springs to Trout Creek is the classic stonefly float.

name: White River
hook: An Ozark tailwater churning out huge numbers of rainbows and true trophy brown trout.
hatches: Year-round fishery: sowbugs and midges daily, streamer fishing on high water for the big browns, shad kills in late winter.
regs: Catch-and-release and special-regulation zones below Bull Shoals Dam — check the current map.
stretches: Bull Shoals Dam down to Cotter; wade at low generation, boat when they're running water.

name: Delaware River, West Branch
hook: The Northeast's most technical dry-fly river — wild rainbows and browns that rise like they've read the books.
hatches: Sulphurs late May–June evenings are prime; BWOs spring and fall, isonychia in summer. Everything depends on reservoir releases.
regs: No-kill catch-and-release on much of the upper West Branch — verify the current boundaries.
stretches: Deposit to Hale Eddy wades well at friendly flows; below Hancock it joins the main stem.

name: Farmington River
hook: New England's most consistent trout stream — bottom-release cold water keeps it fishing all summer.
hatches: Hendricksons April–May, sulphurs in June, tricos and terrestrials late summer, BWOs in fall. Fishes year-round.
regs: The permanent TMA around Riverton and People's State Forest is year-round catch-and-release.
stretches: Riverton down through New Hartford is the heart of it.

name: Missouri River
hook: A giant spring-creek-like tailwater below Holter Dam around Craig — huge wild rainbows and browns in astonishing numbers.
hatches: BWOs and midges spring and fall, the famous PMD and caddis blizzards in June–July, tricos in late summer.
regs: Standard Montana rules; the Craig-to-Cascade water is the classic float — verify current regs.
stretches: Holter Dam to Cascade; wade Craig, or float the ledges and weed beds.

name: Yellowstone River
hook: The longest undammed river in the lower 48 — cutthroat, rainbows and browns through Paradise Valley and the park.
hatches: Mother's Day caddis before runoff, salmonflies and goldens in July, hoppers in August, BWOs in fall.
regs: Yellowstone Park water needs a park permit and is often catch-and-release; verify seasons.
stretches: Paradise Valley below Gardiner floats well; the park water above for cutthroat.

name: Henrys Fork
hook: The Railroad Ranch (Harriman) is the world's most famous — and hardest — dry-fly water for big, picky rainbows.
hatches: Green drakes and PMDs in June, the legendary flav and brown drake hatches, tricos and callibaetis in summer.
regs: The Ranch is flies-only, catch-and-release, no wading in places — check the current rules.
stretches: Harriman State Park (the Ranch) for technical dries; Box Canyon for nymphing big fish.

name: Silver Creek
hook: A glassy spring creek near Sun Valley — a graduate school of dry-fly fishing over huge, wary rainbows and browns.
hatches: Baetis, PMDs, the brown drake spectacle in June, tricos and callibaetis, then hoppers and mahogany duns in fall.
regs: Nature Conservancy preserve water is flies-only, catch-and-release, barbless — verify.
stretches: The Conservancy preserve and Kilpatrick Bridge; long leaders and fine tippet required.

name: Big Hole River
hook: A freestone gem — the last river with a native fluvial grayling, plus browns, rainbows and brookies.
hatches: The salmonfly hatch in late June is the event; caddis, PMDs and hoppers through summer.
regs: Drought closures and hoot-owl restrictions are common in summer — check before you go.
stretches: Wise River to Melrose for the salmonfly float; the upper river for grayling and brookies.

name: North Platte River
hook: The Grey Reef and Miracle Mile tailwaters grow footballs — thick rainbows on a year-round trophy fishery.
hatches: Midges and baetis year-round, leeches and eggs on high water, PMDs and tricos in summer.
regs: Grey Reef has special artificial-only, slot and creel rules — verify the current regs.
stretches: Grey Reef below Alcova for big rainbows; the Miracle Mile between reservoirs.

name: Roaring Fork River
hook: A Gold Medal freestone tumbling from Aspen to Glenwood — browns, rainbows and cutthroat in bouldery pocket water.
hatches: Blue-winged olives and caddis in spring, green drakes and PMDs in summer, big fall BWOs.
regs: Gold Medal water with catch-and-release stretches — check current Colorado rules.
stretches: Basalt to Carbondale for wade and float; the lower canyon to Glenwood for bigger water.

name: Colorado River
hook: The upper Colorado through the canyons above Kremmling and State Bridge — wild browns and rainbows in classic western water.
hatches: BWOs and caddis in spring, PMDs and the famous green drake, hoppers in late summer.
regs: Some Gold Medal / catch-and-release sections — verify the stretch you're on.
stretches: Pumphouse to Radium is the classic float; Gore Canyon below is expert whitewater.

name: Arkansas River
hook: A hundred-plus miles of Gold Medal brown trout water through the Rockies — one of the West's great wade fisheries.
hatches: BWOs in spring, the storied Mother's Day caddis hatch, then PMDs, yellow sallies and hoppers.
regs: Gold Medal from Leadville down; catch-and-release zones near Salida — verify.
stretches: The Buena Vista and Salida stretches wade beautifully; Browns Canyon for float.

name: Provo River
hook: A brown-trout factory tailwater minutes from Salt Lake — the Middle Provo holds thousands of fish per mile.
hatches: Blue-winged olives and midges year-round, PMDs and green drakes in summer, big fall streamer season.
regs: Artificial-only, catch-and-release on much of the Middle and Lower — verify.
stretches: The Middle Provo (Jordanelle to Deer Creek) for wading; the Lower for bigger browns.

name: Yakima River
hook: Washington's only Blue Ribbon trout stream — a desert-canyon river of wild "westslope" cutthroat and rainbows.
hatches: Skwalas kick off spring, then March browns, caddis, PMDs, and the fall October caddis.
regs: Catch-and-release, selective gear rules on the upper river — verify current WDFW regs.
stretches: The Farmlands and the Canyon (Cle Elum to Ellensburg) float well.

name: Deschutes River
hook: A big desert-canyon river with hard-fighting "redside" rainbows and a summer steelhead run.
hatches: The salmonfly and golden stone hatch in May–June is legendary; caddis all summer; steelhead August–November.
regs: No fishing from a floating device — you must anchor or beach and wade.
stretches: The Maupin day water is the easy entry; Warm Springs to Trout Creek is the classic stonefly float.

name: McCloud River
hook: A mossy, spring-fed river below Mt. Shasta — the ancestral home of the McCloud rainbow, wild and beautiful.
hatches: Caddis and PMDs through summer, October caddis in fall, plus a strong stonefly presence.
regs: The Nature Conservancy preserve water is flies-only, catch-and-release, limited rods — verify.
stretches: The Conservancy preserve downstream of Ah-Di-Na; wade the pocket water carefully.

name: Lower Sacramento River
hook: A big tailwater through Redding growing thick, hard-running wild rainbows on a year-round fishery.
hatches: Caddis and BWOs, the famous "bug" fishing on the drift, plus egg patterns behind spawning salmon in fall.
regs: Standard California trout rules; barbless in places — verify.
stretches: Redding to Anderson is prime drift-boat water; the Posse Grounds for wade access.

name: Truckee River
hook: A tough, rewarding freestone between Tahoe and Reno holding big, wild browns and rainbows.
hatches: Baetis and midges, skwalas in spring, then PMDs, caddis and October caddis in fall.
regs: Special-regulation and catch-and-release sections vary by state line — verify CA/NV rules.
stretches: The California water above Truckee wades well; the trophy stretch toward the Nevada line for big browns.

name: Watauga River
hook: A Southern Appalachian tailwater below Wilbur Dam — dense wild browns and stocked rainbows in cold, clear water.
hatches: Sulphurs and caddis in spring and summer, midges and BWOs year-round, terrestrials late summer.
regs: Quality/trophy sections with gear limits — verify current TWRA rules and generation schedules.
stretches: Below Wilbur Dam down toward Elizabethton; watch dam-release flows.

name: South Holston River
hook: One of the Southeast's best — a bug-factory tailwater famous for its all-day sulphur hatch and fat wild browns.
hatches: Sulphurs virtually year-round, BWOs, midges, and a strong caddis presence.
regs: Trophy trout regs with slot limits — verify TWRA rules and the dam generation schedule before wading.
stretches: Below South Holston Dam to Bluff City; the weir pool and the flats.

name: Davidson River
hook: A gorgeous Pisgah Forest stream with famously educated, well-fed wild trout below the fish hatchery.
hatches: BWOs and midges year-round, caddis and Light Cahills in spring, terrestrials in summer.
regs: The hatchery section is catch-and-release, flies-only — verify current rules.
stretches: The catch-and-release water around the hatchery; go small and fine.

name: Housatonic River
hook: A big western-Connecticut river with wild and holdover trout up top and excellent smallmouth in the warmer reaches.
hatches: Hendricksons and the famous "Hendrickson" and tan caddis in spring, then sulphurs, isonychia, and fall BWOs.
regs: The TMA (trout management area) has catch-and-release, artificial-only rules — verify.
stretches: The Cornwall-to-Kent TMA for trout; the lower river for summer smallmouth.

name: Beaverkill River
hook: The birthplace of American dry-fly fishing in the Catskills — hallowed pools and wild-plus-stocked browns.
hatches: Hendricksons and Quill Gordons in spring, March browns, sulphurs, then tricos and isonychia in summer.
regs: No-kill catch-and-release sections around Roscoe — verify current DEC rules.
stretches: The famous no-kill water at Junction Pool in Roscoe and downstream.

name: Penns Creek
hook: Central Pennsylvania's legendary limestone-influenced stream — big wild browns and the mythical green drake hatch.
hatches: Grannoms in April, sulphurs in May, and the famous green drake "coffin fly" spectacle in late May–June.
regs: Catch-and-release / all-tackle trophy sections vary — verify current PA rules.
stretches: The Broadwater and the catch-and-release stretch below Coburn.

name: Gunpowder River
hook: A cold tailwater below Prettyboy just north of Baltimore — thousands of wild browns in an intimate freestone setting.
hatches: BWOs and midges year-round, Hendricksons and caddis in spring, sulphurs and tricos into summer.
regs: Catch-and-release, artificial-only on the trophy water — verify current Maryland DNR rules.
stretches: Below Prettyboy Dam through the Falls Road area; small flies, careful wading.

name: Gallatin River
hook: A classic wade-fishing freestone (yes, the "A River Runs Through It" river) with willing rainbows, browns and cutthroat.
hatches: Salmonflies and goldens in July, PMDs and caddis all summer, hoppers in August, fall BWOs.
regs: No fishing from a boat on much of it — a wader's river. Verify Montana rules.
stretches: The canyon between Big Sky and Gallatin Gateway is roadside pocket-water heaven.

name: Snake River
hook: Under the Tetons near Jackson — home of the beautiful, aggressive native fine-spotted Snake River cutthroat.
hatches: PMDs and caddis in summer, the gray/green drakes, and the famous late-summer hopper and attractor dry fishing.
regs: Cutthroat are largely catch-and-release; verify Wyoming seasons and the park boundaries.
stretches: Float the classic stretches below Jackson Lake Dam through the canyon.

name: Blackfoot River
hook: A rugged, scenic freestone east of Missoula — wild trout in a wilderness feel, with a strong native cutthroat presence.
hatches: Salmonflies in June, then PMDs, caddis, spruce moths and hoppers through summer.
regs: Catch-and-release on cutthroat and bull trout; hoot-owl closures in hot spells — verify.
stretches: The roadside water along Highway 200; float the canyon sections.

name: Bitterroot River
hook: A braided valley river south of Missoula famous for the earliest dry-fly action in the West — the skwala hatch.
hatches: Skwala stoneflies in March–April, then March browns, PMDs, caddis, and green drakes.
regs: Standard Montana rules with some closures — verify current regs.
stretches: The West Fork and the main stem through the Bitterroot Valley.

name: Metolius River
hook: A spring creek that erupts full-grown from the ground near Camp Sherman — crystalline water and famously selective redband trout and bull trout.
hatches: Green drakes and caddis in spring/summer, BWOs, and technical midge fishing year-round.
regs: Flies-only, catch-and-release; wild and demanding — verify ODFW rules.
stretches: From the headwaters spring down past Camp Sherman; sight-fish the flats.

name: Grande Ronde River
hook: A remote canyon river straddling Oregon and Washington — one of the classic dry-line summer steelhead swings.
hatches: Summer steelhead in fall; the trout fishing (redbands and bull trout) is strong in warmer months too.
regs: Steelhead rules and wild-release requirements vary by state line — verify carefully.
stretches: The lower canyon toward Troy for swinging flies in October.

name: Klamath River
hook: A big Northern California river with a storied "half-pounder" summer steelhead run — smaller, aggressive fish that eat swung flies.
hatches: Steelhead from late summer into fall; caddis and October caddis bring them up.
regs: Steelhead regulations and low-flow closures apply — verify current CDFW rules.
stretches: The middle river around Happy Camp and the Salmon River confluence.

name: Owens River
hook: An Eastern Sierra gem below Mammoth — the Upper Owens holds big migratory rainbows and browns from Crowley Reservoir.
hatches: BWOs and midges, caddis and PMDs in summer, plus big fall runs of Crowley fish.
regs: Wild trout and special-regulation sections — verify CDFW rules.
stretches: The Upper Owens above Crowley for migratory fish; the Wild Trout section in the gorge.

name: Green River
hook: The upper Green in the Wind River country — high-desert freestone water with wild browns, rainbows and cutthroat.
hatches: Salmonflies and goldens early summer, PMDs, tricos and hoppers later.
regs: Standard Wyoming rules; some special sections — verify.
stretches: Around Pinedale and Warren Bridge; float or wade the riffle-run water.

name: Watauga & Elk
hook: High-country wild-trout water in the North Carolina mountains — small, tumbling streams full of colorful brookies and browns.
hatches: Quill Gordons and Blue Quills in early spring, then caddis, sulphurs and summer terrestrials.
regs: Wild trout / catch-and-release sections vary — verify NCWRC rules.
stretches: The headwater freestones around Banner Elk; high-stick the pockets.

name: Toccoa River
hook: North Georgia's premier tailwater below Blue Ridge Dam — dense stocked rainbows and wild browns in cold mountain water.
hatches: Caddis and sulphurs in spring/summer, BWOs and midges cool months, terrestrials in late summer.
regs: Standard Georgia trout rules; watch the dam generation schedule before wading.
stretches: The tailwater below Blue Ridge Dam toward McCaysville.

name: South Holston & Clinch
hook: A Tennessee tailwater below Norris Dam near Knoxville — trophy wild browns and rainbows in cold, clear water.
hatches: Sulphurs in May–June, BWOs and midges year-round, and a famous fall run of big browns.
regs: Trophy/slot regulations and generation schedules — verify TWRA rules before wading.
stretches: Below Norris Dam through Miller's Island; fish the low-water windows.

name: Namekagon River
hook: A classic Northwoods river — coldwater trout up top giving way to excellent smallmouth downstream, all in wild Wisconsin forest.
hatches: Hendricksons and the famous Hex hatch on the trout water in June; smallmouth on top all summer below.
regs: Trout regulations on the upper river; verify Wisconsin DNR rules.
stretches: The upper river above Hayward for trout; the lower for smallmouth floats.

name: Root River
hook: The heart of Minnesota's Driftless — spring-fed limestone streams winding through bluff country, thick with wild brown trout.
hatches: BWOs and midges early, caddis and sulphurs in spring/summer, tricos and terrestrials late.
regs: Special-regulation and catch-and-release stretches throughout the Driftless — verify.
stretches: The South Branch and the many spring creeks around Lanesboro and Preston.

name: Norfork River
hook: A short, intense Ozark tailwater below Norfork Dam — huge trout in cold water, a trophy brown factory alongside the White.
hatches: Sowbugs and midges every day, BWOs, and streamer fishing on high water for the big browns.
regs: Catch-and-release and special-regulation zones below the dam — verify Arkansas rules.
stretches: The few miles below Norfork Dam to the White River confluence.

name: Current River
hook: A spring-fed Ozark river of stunning clarity — the Montauk and Baptist Camp trout water plus wild smallmouth downstream.
hatches: Midges and BWOs, caddis and sulphurs in spring/summer, scuds and cress bugs year-round.
regs: Blue/red/white ribbon areas with different gear rules — verify Missouri Dept. of Conservation rules.
stretches: The trophy trout stretch below Montauk State Park; the wild river for smallmouth.

name: Guadalupe River
hook: The southernmost year-round trout tailwater in the US, below Canyon Lake in the Hill Country — a winter stocked fishery with holdovers.
hatches: Midges and small mayflies in winter/spring; the river warms to a bass and Rio Grande cichlid fishery in summer.
regs: Special-regulation trout zones below the dam — verify Texas Parks & Wildlife rules.
stretches: The tailrace below Canyon Dam; fish the cool water November through spring.
